title: Eschatology: The Great Tribulation — Part 1
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Lesson 9 on Eschatology_ The Great Tribulation - Part 1.txt

outline.title: The Order of Events in Historic Covenantal Premillennialism
subpoints: A future Great Tribulation — the visible church will go through it, not be spared
The physical, bodily return of Christ after the tribulation
Resurrection of the righteous at Christ's return
The new heavens and new earth — recreation of the world
A literal thousand-year physical reign of Christ
Rebellion of unbelievers led by Satan at the close of the millennium
Resurrection of unbelievers and the final judgment
The eternal state

outline.title: Three Tribulation Systems and the Three Reform Positions
subpoints: Pre-tribulation: Christ returns before the tribulation to remove the church — associated with dispensationalism; all three Reformed positions reject this
Mid-tribulation: Christ returns halfway through to spare the church from the second half of God's wrath
Post-tribulation: Christ returns after the tribulation — all three Reformed systems (amillennial, postmillennial, historic premillennial) hold this view

The post-tribulation position still allows for the church to be shielded from God's wrath during judgment — because the seven seals, trumpets, and bowls of Revelation are concurrent; the sevens all conclude with the return of Christ
WLC Q. 86 (amended): the catechism jumps over any notion of tribulation; it is not addressed in the Westminster Standards because the assembly itself was divided on eschatology

outline.title: The Olivet Discourse — Matthew 24:15-31
subpoints: Context: Thursday night before the crucifixion; the disciples question Jesus about the temple's destruction from the Mount of Olives (the Olivet Discourse)
The Olivet Discourse parallels are Matthew 24-25, Mark 13, and Luke 17/21 — the three synoptic gospels
John does not include the Olivet Discourse; instead John 13-17 is the Upper Room Discourse delivered the same evening

Matthew 24:15-31 read: the abomination of desolation, flight from Judea, the unprecedented Great Tribulation, false Christs, the darkening of sun and moon, the sign of the Son of Man, and the gathering of the elect
This chapter is known as the Olivet Discourse because it was delivered on the Mount of Olives
The red-letter edition note that verse 15 contains an editorial comment from Matthew: 'let the reader understand'

outline.title: Three Ways of Interpreting Revelation 6-19 (and the Olivet Discourse)
subpoints: Preterist view: all events in Revelation 6-19 have already happened — primarily fulfilled in the Neronian persecution (AD 54-68) and the destruction of Jerusalem (AD 66-70); held by most amillennialists and postmillennialists

Historicist view: Revelation 6-19 describes a historical development from the first coming to the second coming; dispensationalists and some amillennialists; problem is that identifications of seals/trumpets with specific historical events require constant adjustment
Futurist view: Revelation 6-19 describes events still future to us; held by the pastor and some amillennialists (notably William Hendriksen)
Each view has serious proponents; the pastor has not yet proved anything — only mapping the landscape

outline.title: Arguments for the Preterist Position (and Why They Fall Short)
subpoints: References to Judea and flight from Jerusalem in Matthew 24:16 suggest a local event — consistent with AD 70
'This generation shall not pass away until all these things take place' (Matthew 24:34) — suggests fulfillment in the first century
Desecration of the temple (24:15) implies the temple is still standing — consistent with a pre-AD 70 writing
BUT: Matthew 24:29 says the return of the Son of Man comes 'immediately after' the tribulation — if the tribulation was AD 70, Christ should have returned then

BUT: Matthew 24:21 — this tribulation will be unlike anything since the beginning of the world, greater than the flood — clearly more than a local persecution in Rome and the fall of one city in Palestine

BUT: Matthew 24:31 — immediately following the tribulation, the angels gather the elect at the sound of a trumpet — this is the resurrection language of 1 Thessalonians 4, not something that happened in AD 70
Revelation's date: if written in the 90s AD (during Domitian's reign, as testified by Irenaeus and others), it could not be prophesying the fall of Jerusalem in 70 AD

summary:
Having established the covenant unity of Israel and the church over the previous three lessons, Pastor Tito Lyro now turns to the specific prophetic events surrounding the return of Christ. This lesson opens by laying out the order of events in historic covenantal premillennialism: a future Great Tribulation the church will pass through, followed by the bodily return of Christ, the resurrection of the righteous, the recreation of the world, a thousand-year physical reign of Christ, a final rebellion, the resurrection of the unrighteous, final judgment, and the eternal state.

The pastor surveys three tribulation-timing systems: pre-tribulation (Christ removes the church before it begins), mid-tribulation (Christ returns at the midpoint), and post-tribulation (Christ returns after it ends). He notes that all three Reformed eschatological positions — amillennial, postmillennial, and historic premillennial — agree on a post-tribulational return of Christ. The pre-tribulation rapture is associated exclusively with dispensationalism, which the BPC does not permit for ordained officers. The Westminster Standards are silent on the tribulation because the Westminster Assembly was itself divided on eschatological matters.

The Olivet Discourse — Matthew 24-25 (with parallels in Mark 13 and Luke 21) — is read in detail. The discourse was delivered on the Mount of Olives, Thursday evening before the crucifixion, in response to the disciples' questions about the temple. Pastor Lyro then surveys the three main interpretive approaches to Revelation 6-19: preterism (all fulfilled in the first century, primarily in AD 70), historicism (a running historical allegory from the first to second coming), and futurism (describing events still future). The pastor holds the futurist position.

The lesson concludes by examining the preterist arguments — the references to Judea, 'this generation,' and the standing temple — and showing that several elements in Matthew 24 cannot fit a first-century fulfillment: the return of Christ is said to come immediately after the tribulation (v. 29), the tribulation is described as the greatest in world history (v. 21), and the gathering of the elect with a trumpet blast (v. 31) is clearly the language of the final resurrection, not AD 70. Furthermore, if the book of Revelation was written in the AD 90s under Domitian — as testified by Irenaeus and other early fathers — it cannot be prophesying events that had already occurred twenty years earlier.
transcript:
## Introduction and Order of Events

The light my scary slide about the great tribulation was the font note to make it so it all on the graphics that's how you get things going here. I actually like you to grab a Bible and turn to Matthew 24. Start verse 15. So the gospel of Matthew, chapter 24, verse 15. This chapter — what's in Matthew 24 and some of 25 — is also found in Mark 13 and Luke 21. So these are the three parallel passages and what we're going to read here is what's being called the Olivet Discourse because it was delivered on the Mount of Olives.

To set up for you: the disciples and Jesus had left Jerusalem on that Thursday night and they likely had crossed the Kidron Valley which is you go down and up to the Mount of Olives and they had gotten up on the Mount of Olives just enough to see over the wall and be able to actually see the temple, because the Olivet Discourse starts with a question by the disciples regarding what Jesus thought about the destruction of the temple. So Jesus is answering that question. That's Thursday evening — they're looking back and they've seen the temple in its glory, they asked the question, and Jesus is answering them. And you find that answer again in Matthew 24, Mark 13, and Luke 21 — and those are what's called the three synoptic gospels. Synoptic means seen together — they are very parallel so you can actually see them together; there's a lot of the same things in all three gospels.

John doesn't include this discourse. Instead of including the Olivet Discourse that we're going to read today, John includes what's been called the Upper Room Discourse in John 13 — well, we consider it to 17, though they leave the upper room in the beginning of chapter 15. So two discourses delivered in the same night, just within an hour or so from each other, and there are two great discourses on the last night of Jesus before he's crucified.

So we're going to start reading in verse 15 and then we're going to come back and address these things. My Bible has an editor's title to that section called the Great Tribulation — and that's what your Bible does as well. You know that's serious stuff because it's in red — just kidding, red letters don't mean more important.

Verse 15: therefore when you see the abomination of desolation spoken of by Daniel the prophet standing in the holy place — whoever reads let him understand — then let those who are in Judea flee to the mountains. Let him who is on the housetop not go down to take anything out of his house, and let him who is in the field not go back to get his clothes. But woe to those who are pregnant and to those who are nursing babies in those days. And pray that your flight may not be in winter or on the Sabbath. For then there will be great tribulation such as has not been since the beginning of the world until this time, no, nor ever shall be. And unless those days were shortened, no flesh would be saved. But for the elect's sake those days will be shortened. Then if anyone says to you, 'Look, here is the Christ!' or 'There!' do not believe it. For false Christs and false prophets will rise and show great signs and wonders to deceive, if possible, even the elect. See, I have told you beforehand. Therefore if they say to you, 'Look, He is in the desert!' do not go out; or 'Look, He is in the inner rooms!' do not believe it. For as the lightning comes from the east and flashes to the west, so also will the coming of the Son of Man be. For wherever the carcass is, there the eagles will be gathered together.

We're going to stop here and we actually going to consider the whole chapter hopefully by the time we are done with our lesson today.

## Order of Events in Historic Premillennialism

I hope you're enjoying this series as much as I've enjoyed preparing it. And I think that enjoyment has actually lengthened the series which seems to be something that all my series suffered from — getting lengthened through the preparation.

Today we're going to start looking at the nitty gritty of historic or covenantal premillennialism, and as a reminder this is not a debate — we're not going to be debating things, so I'm going to be teaching and you're going to be learning. If you have questions you want to know just to understand me on some area better, I'm happy to take those. We're not going to carry a debate on the different positions, though I'm more than happy to do that with you at a different time, not during Sunday school.

As we start looking at the nitty gritty of historic or covenantal premillennialism, I want to start by looking at how this view of the end times lays out the order of events. I stayed away from graphics because that associates too much with dispensational premillennialism — I didn't want any connection even though timelines are helpful.

This view sees a Great Tribulation in the future — ahead of us — a tribulation through which the visible church will go. The church is not going to be spared from that. This view also sees that after the tribulation, the Christ's return — and that will be a bodily, physical return, not a spiritual, not a figurative return. And at that point, the believers will be raised at the return of Christ. That will be followed by the new heavens and the new earth, so the recreation of the world. And then a thousand-year physical reign, followed by rebellion of unbelievers led by Satan, then the resurrection of unbelievers, then the eternal final judgment and the eternal state.

So that's the order that this particular view understands the Bible, and that's what we're going to be following as we continue our study. Today we're going to try to take a look at this first item — the tribulation that is still future to the church.

## Three Tribulation Systems

There have been three different tribulation systems suggested, and the Westminster symbols don't mention anything about the tribulation of any sort. One of the reasons is that in some ways the Westminster Confession and Catechisms are a consensus document — they're not going to address some things because there was a lot of difference in the assembly themselves about eschatology. A third of the assembly was pretty well postmillennial; I know a third of the assembly was definitely amillennial and there was a few of each position there. So they didn't put anything in about the tribulation.

The closest we get to it is WLC Q. 86 which we've already studied. And you notice that it just blows right through any notion of tribulation. So from that perspective, the standards are not going to be as helpful for this portion of our study.

There have been these three systems. One is that Christ returns before the tribulation in order to remove the church from the scene prior to the outpouring of God's wrath upon the nations. That's what we call the pre-tribulation return of Christ — Christ returns for the church before the tribulation, removes the church from the world, the nations are judged, the wrath of God is poured upon the nations, but the church is not around for that.

There's another view — the mid-tribulation return of Christ — Christ is going to return halfway through the tribulation. He does that to remove the church from the brunt of the pouring of God's wrath. So God's not pouring as much wrath in the first half, but God's going to remove the church from the judgment of the second half of the tribulation.

And then the third one is that Christ returns after the tribulation. That's a post-tribulation return of Christ. All three Reformed eschatological systems — amillennialism, postmillennialism, and historic premillennialism — hold to this view of a post-tribulational coming of Christ. Christ comes after the tribulation that's described in the New Testament and in some passages of the Old Testament.

This position still allows for the church to be shielded from the outpouring of God's wrath in a couple different ways. One is — if you look at the book of Revelation, you have seven seals, then you have seven trumpets, and you have seven bowls. The seventh of each series lines up. So the book of Revelation doesn't have the seven seals happening and then when they're done, the seven trumpets, and then the seven bowls — they're kind of happening at the same time. The numbers don't all match up, but the sevens do. They're concurrently happening. And the sevens seem to be the coming of Christ in all each one of them. When Christ comes to pour his ultimate wrath, the church is with him. So the church doesn't bear the brunt of God's wrath with the nations as he pours it out.

## The Olivet Discourse

These are the major passages related to the Great Tribulation. And we'll also talk about 1 and 2 Thessalonians in relation to these four passages — but this is the four major passages regarding this tribulation: Matthew 24-25, Mark 13, Luke 21, and Revelation 6-19.

The next thing I wanted to show to you is that this Great Tribulation described in the Bible is still future to us. There are three ways of thinking about the prophecies of the Old Testament as they relate to the New Testament and the book of Revelation.

One is the preterist way — that is, that all the events for example in Revelation 6 through 19 have already happened, together with most of the prophecies in the Old and New Testaments. And Revelation 6 through 19 is primarily speaking of the fall of Jerusalem in the year AD 70. So everything you see there has been already accomplished with some elements of chapter 19 being spilled over to a later time. That's the preterist position — preterist means past. Amillennialists will hold to this position, postmillennialists will go to this position. There's one big exception in the amillennial camp: William Hendriksen — he was a major New Testament scholar and he did not hold to this position. He saw the tribulation as still future to us.

Another way to see it is that Revelation 6 to 19 is a historical development of the time between the first coming of Christ and the second coming of Christ — those chapters describing the world and the heavens and interactions between the first and second coming of Christ. Some people have tried to identify periods of history that would fit with the trumpets, with the bowls, etc. The problem with that is that you can't really tie them one-to-one, and the longer history goes the more you have to readjust your views. This has historically been more of a view that dispensational people or some amillennialists hold to.

And then the third one is to look at Revelation 6 and 19 as future events — events that are still future to us. That's the view I hold. Any questions about these three ways of thinking of Revelation 6 through 19?

## Preterist Arguments and Their Problems

So, continuing: amillennialists and postmillennialists link the Great Tribulation with the Neronian persecution and the fall of Jerusalem in AD 67-70. Nero ruled from AD 54 through AD 68 and was a capable ruler for the first five years of his reign. Something happened in AD 59 that made him become a raging maniac. He started then — he murdered most of his family including his mother, kicked his wife to death, murdered his teacher Seneca. He wasted the treasury of Rome. And it was during this later portion of his reign that he persecuted the Christians in Rome, especially after AD 64 when about two-thirds of Rome was burned. There's a fire that burnt two-thirds of Rome, and he accused the Christians of having set the fire to divert attention from him, because most people thought he started the fire because he wanted to rebuild Rome and call it 'Nereopolis' — the city of Nero.

Historians are now convinced that Nero started the fire and found a scapegoat in the Christians. He started doing all kinds of different things to them. Christians were burned alive on crosses — not just to execute them, but for entertainment at his parties. On party days, he would gather up Christians, crucify them along the road leading to the palace and light them on fire to burn alive to provide lighting for the guests. So the persecution was terrible. But it was extremely localized — it did not spill beyond Rome.

At the same time, around the end of Nero's reign, the Jews in Palestine rebelled — probably around AD 65-66. Rome dispatched one of their most famous generals, Vespasian, with his legions to put down that rebellion. Vespasian went and besieged Jerusalem. And there were four years they stayed around Jerusalem — indications that people were actually eating dead people in order to stay alive in Jerusalem. Eventually Nero died, there was a period of confusion, Vespasian was recalled to Rome, marched in with his legions, and became the new emperor. He left his son Titus to be the general to overtake Jerusalem. And in AD 70, Titus decided to breach the walls and enter Jerusalem, and it was the most terrible destruction of Jerusalem.

Now these years were of great suffering for the Jewish people. Most Christians had already left Jerusalem — as we read in Acts chapter 8, following Saul's persecution, Jerusalem Christians mostly left Jerusalem and went to Samaria and other regions outside of Judea.

This interpretation — that the tribulation was the fall of Jerusalem and Nero's persecution — does fit with some of what Jesus says in the synoptic gospels. For example, in Matthew 24:16 we have all kinds of references to Judea and Palestine: 'Then let those who are in Judea flee to the mountains.' Jesus uses this reference to Judea, giving the impression that he's referring to things happening specifically in Judea, which would fit the interpretation of the destruction of Jerusalem.

There's also a reference to the generation listening to Jesus not passing away — verse 34 of Matthew 24: 'I assure you that this generation will by no means pass away until all these things take place.' There's also a reference to the desecration of the temple — meaning there needs to be a temple standing. So it does fit with the idea of the temple still being around.

However, there are many other elements from the synoptics that don't fit this interpretation. For example, in verses 29 and 30 of Matthew 24, the return of Christ happens immediately after the tribulation described in the gospel: 'Immediately after the tribulation of those days the sun will be darkened, and the moon will not give its light, the stars will fall from heaven, and the powers of the heavens will be shaken. Then the sign of the Son of Man will appear in heaven, and then all the tribes of the earth will mourn, and they will see the Son of Man coming in the clouds of heaven with power and great glory.'

So you can see that in Jesus' account, the return of the Son of Man happens immediately following the tribulation. And amillennialists and postmillennialists have understood the tension here and try to redefine this word 'coming.' They say that this 'coming' means a spiritual coming as in the coming of the Spirit or something like that. The problem is that the word 'coming' throughout the New Testament is used for physical coming — the word parousia is the most common word for coming or going, physical coming or going. And that's how it's used throughout the book of Matthew. So if that's not what he meant here, it would be the only time in which Jesus meant that word in a different way than all the other times Matthew used it.

Another problem with seeing Matthew 24 as referring only to the fall of Jerusalem or Nero's persecution is that this tribulation is supposed to be the biggest turmoil in the history of the world. Look at verses 21 and 22: 'For then there will be great tribulation, such as has not been since the beginning of the world until this time, no, nor ever shall be.' It says the greatest tribulation ever — greater than the flood, because it says 'it's never happened in this magnitude.' Well, the flood happened before, so it must be greater than the flood. Greater than the captivity and exile where the entire nation of Israel was taken away. So you can see that doesn't quite fit the destruction of one city in Palestine and the localized, terrible persecution of Christians in Rome under Nero.

And then one last thing that doesn't fit: verse 31: 'And He will send His angels with a great sound of a trumpet, and they will gather together His elect from the four winds, from one end of heaven to the other.' Where do we hear again the trumpet being sounded and the saints being called from all corners of the earth? In 1 Thessalonians — which is a reference to the resurrection of the body. So this interpretation that the Great Tribulation was the destruction of Jerusalem and the persecution of Nero doesn't fit what Jesus says here in Matthew 24, and in Mark 13 and Luke 21.

And it also does not fit Revelation 6 through 19. If you read Revelation 6 and 19 — which we're not going to do this morning, it would actually lengthen the series quite a bit — Revelation 6 and 19 speaks of a cosmic event that goes beyond a local persecution in Rome and the destruction of an ancient city in Palestine. Jerusalem might be significant to us as Christians and significant to the Jews, but worldwide at the time it's very insignificant.

Another thing that makes the preterist interpretation not match Revelation 6 through 19 is that the book of Revelation itself had not been written by AD 70 — so it could not be prophesying something that had already happened.

When was Revelation written? We know it was written when John was exiled on Patmos. Irenaeus — a church father writing in AD 180 — said that John was in Patmos during the reign of Emperor Domitian and this becomes important. And Irenaeus is especially important since he is in direct line from the apostle John: John had a disciple and student named Polycarp, and Polycarp was Irenaeus's teacher. So you can see just one generation of direct connection with John.

This is what Irenaeus says: 'For that was seen no very long time since, but almost in our day, towards the end of Domitian's reign' — talking about when Revelation was written. So it was written in the AD 80s or 90s. And Domitian reigned from AD 81 to 96.

Now a chronology of the first-century Roman emperors: Augustus is the one of Jesus's birth; Tiberius is named in the gospels; Gaius, most people know as Caligula, probably one of the most depraved of all the emperors; Claudius — the one who kicked all the Jews out of Rome, so Priscilla and Aquila left and met Paul; Nero — the last of the Julio-Claudians; then a period of confusion with Galba, Otho, and Vitellius before Vespasian came and began the Flavian dynasty. Then his son Titus for two years, then Domitian from AD 81 to 96.

Remember: Irenaeus says John wrote Revelation towards the end of Domitian's reign. So somewhere in the 90s. The external evidence tells us Revelation was written after the Neronian persecution and after the destruction of Jerusalem. Therefore it could not be prophesying those events.

You might ask: how come there are so many intelligent people who think Revelation was written before AD 70? Well, the reason is they look at the book of Revelation and they see enough internal evidence to decide that Irenaeus got the date wrong. And they say the internal evidence fits better with a pre-AD 70 writing. I wouldn't agree with that, but I think it's important that we understand that that view is based on scholarship, not ignorance. In spite of those who have argued for a date before AD 70, the testimony of Irenaeus and other fathers which places the writing towards the end of Domitian's reign has convinced those who hold to the futurist position that Revelation was written in the 90s. And if that's the case, Revelation was written after the destruction of Jerusalem and cannot be prophesying it.

Any questions before we continue? Jerry, how do you address the 'this generation shall not pass' statement? It's funny because that's exactly the next point of our notes which we'll have to cover next time because of time. But I'll just tease you by saying: think about how the Bible uses prophecy in the Old Testament — prophecy often describes a thing as a whole without separating the parts. Often it prophesies by looking from the end — from the accomplished. So you look from the end and it looks like you only have one mountain, but when you turn and look from the profile you see that there's distance and there are two mountains. So it's possible that the synoptic Olivet Discourse is talking about both the destruction of Jerusalem and the future tribulation, and that's why we have these different references there.

All right, any last question before we close and pray? All right. Father, thank you so much that you are good to us. We thank you that you have given grace to your church to be able to see these things and agree on some points and yet keep on pursuing the truth and fellowship with one another. We pray that you would use this truth — that we all agree Christ is returning, the blessed hope of our resurrection — to cause us to be ready to worship you this morning. That we might join the saints of all the ages in praising you, your Son, and your Spirit. In Jesus' name, Amen.
